---
title: C++ API reference
toc: true
toc_icon: file-code
---

**Note:** All C++ code shown here is generated by `lrpcg`. You do not write it — you implement the abstract methods it declares.
{: .notice}

## Overview

`lrpcg cpp` generates a set of header files for each interface definition. For a definition named `example` with services `math` and `sensor`, the output looks like:

``` text
generated/
  example/
    example.hpp          ← top-level include (pulls in everything)
    math_shim.hpp        ← abstract base for the math service
    sensor_shim.hpp      ← abstract base for the sensor service
    example_includes.hpp ← forward declarations and type definitions
    math_includes.hpp
    sensor_includes.hpp
```

You only need to include `example.hpp` in your application.

In addition, `lrpcg cppcore` generates the static core files that do not depend on any definition:

``` text
generated/
  lrpccore/
    Server.hpp
    Service.hpp
    LrpcTypes.hpp
    EtlRwExtensions.hpp
```

## Server class

The top-level include exposes a typedef for the fully configured server:

``` cpp
// example.hpp (generated)
namespace ex {
    using example = lrpc::Server<...>;
}
```

`lrpc::Server` is a template class parameterized with buffer sizes and the meta service. You interact with it through the following interface:

### lrpcTransmit (pure virtual)

``` cpp
virtual void lrpcTransmit(lrpc::span<const uint8_t> bytes) = 0;
```

You **must** subclass the generated server and implement this method. LotusRPC calls it whenever it has a frame ready to send to the client. Wire it to your hardware transmit routine (UART, SPI, TCP socket, etc.).

``` cpp
class MyServer : public ex::example {
    void lrpcTransmit(lrpc::span<const uint8_t> bytes) override {
        uart_write(bytes.data(), bytes.size());
    }
};
```

### registerService

``` cpp
void registerService(Service& service);
```

Registers a service instance with the server. Call once per service before processing any data. The service object must outlive the server.

### lrpcReceive

``` cpp
void lrpcReceive(lrpc::span<const uint8_t> bytes);
void lrpcReceive(uint8_t byte);
```

Feeds incoming bytes to the server. Call from your receive interrupt or polling loop. LotusRPC handles framing internally — pass bytes as they arrive.

## Service shim class

For each service in the definition, `lrpcg` generates a shim class. You derive from it and implement its pure virtual methods.

For a service named `math` with one function `add(int32_t a, int32_t b) -> int32_t`:

``` cpp
// math_shim.hpp (generated)
namespace ex {
    class math_shim : public lrpc::Service {
    protected:
        virtual int32_t add(int32_t a, int32_t b) = 0;
    };
}
```

Your implementation:

``` cpp
class MathService : public ex::math_shim {
protected:
    int32_t add(int32_t a, int32_t b) override {
        return a + b;
    }
};
```

## Type mapping

The table below shows how LRPC definition types map to C++ types in function parameters and return values.

| LRPC type                 | C++ parameter type    | C++ return type           |
|---------------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| `uint8_t`, `int8_t`, etc. | by value              | by value                  |
| `float`, `double`         | by value              | by value                  |
| `bool`                    | by value              | by value                  |
| enum                      | by value              | by value                  |
| struct                    | `const MyStruct&`     | `MyStruct` (by value)     |
| `string` / `string_N`     | `lrpc::string_view`   | `lrpc::string_view`       |
| `bytearray`               | `lrpc::bytearray`     | `lrpc::bytearray`         |
| array (count N)           | `lrpc::span<const T>` | `lrpc::span<const T>`     |
| optional                  | `lrpc::optional<T>`   | `lrpc::optional<T>`       |
| multiple returns          | —                     | `std::tuple<T1, T2, ...>` |

**Lifetime note:** `lrpc::string_view`, `lrpc::bytearray` and `lrpc::span` are non-owning views. As parameters they are valid for the duration of the function call. As return values, the viewed data must remain valid until LotusRPC has finished encoding it into the transmit buffer.
{: .notice--warning}

For a detailed explanation of ownership semantics, see [Settings reference — Ownership](reference_settings#parameter-and-return-value-ownership).

### Type aliases

| Alias               | Underlying type               |
|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| `lrpc::string_view` | `etl::string_view`            |
| `lrpc::bytearray`   | `etl::span<const lrpc::byte>` |
| `lrpc::span<T>`     | `etl::span<T>`                |
| `lrpc::array<T, N>` | `std::array<T, N>`            |
| `lrpc::optional<T>` | `etl::optional<T>`            |

## Functions

### Single return value

``` yaml
functions:
  - name: get_temperature
    returns:
      - { name: temp, type: float }
```

Generated shim method:

``` cpp
virtual float get_temperature() = 0;
```

Implementation:

``` cpp
float get_temperature() override { return read_sensor(); }
```

### Multiple return values

``` yaml
functions:
  - name: get_status
    returns_alias: Status
    returns:
      - { name: code, type: uint8_t }
      - { name: message, type: string }
```

Generated shim method:

``` cpp
using Status = std::tuple<uint8_t, lrpc::string_view>;
virtual Status get_status() = 0;
```

Implementation:

``` cpp
Status get_status() override {
    return {0, "OK"};
}
```

## Streams

### Client stream

The client sends data to the server. The server receives it through a pure virtual method and can optionally request the client to stop.

``` yaml
streams:
  - name: log_data
    origin: client
    finite: true
    params:
      - { name: entry, type: string }
```

Generated shim methods:

``` cpp
// Called for each incoming message. `final` is true for the last message (finite streams only).
virtual void log_data(lrpc::string_view entry, bool final) = 0;

// Call this to ask the client to stop sending (optional).
void log_data_requestStop();
```

Implementation:

``` cpp
void log_data(lrpc::string_view entry, bool final) override {
    store_log(entry);
    if (final) flush_log();
}
```

### Server stream

The client initiates and terminates the stream. The server sends data back by calling a non-virtual response method.

``` yaml
streams:
  - name: sensor_data
    origin: server
    params:
      - { name: value, type: uint16_t }
```

Generated shim methods:

``` cpp
// Called when the client sends 'start'.
virtual void sensor_data() = 0;

// Called when the client sends 'stop'.
virtual void sensor_data_stop() = 0;

// Call this to send a data message to the client.
void sensor_data_response(uint16_t value);
```

Implementation:

``` cpp
void sensor_data() override {
    streaming_ = true;
}

void sensor_data_stop() override {
    streaming_ = false;
}

// Somewhere in your application loop:
void on_new_measurement(uint16_t v) {
    if (streaming_) sensor_data_response(v);
}
```

### Finite server stream

For a finite server stream, the response method has an additional `bool final` parameter:

``` cpp
void sensor_data_response(uint16_t value, bool final);
```

Pass `final = true` with the last message to signal end of stream.

## Complete example

This example shows a full server setup with one service, two functions, and a UART-based transport.

**Definition** (`math.lrpc.yaml`):

``` yaml
name: math
settings:
  namespace: ex
services:
  - name: calc
    functions:
      - name: add
        params:
          - { name: a, type: int32_t }
          - { name: b, type: int32_t }
        returns:
          - { name: result, type: int32_t }
      - name: multiply
        params:
          - { name: a, type: int32_t }
          - { name: b, type: int32_t }
        returns:
          - { name: result, type: int32_t }
```

**Service implementation** (`CalcService.hpp`):

``` cpp
#include "math/math.hpp"

class CalcService : public ex::calc_shim {
protected:
    int32_t add(int32_t a, int32_t b) override { return a + b; }
    int32_t multiply(int32_t a, int32_t b) override { return a * b; }
};
```

**Server with UART transport** (`main.cpp`):

``` cpp
#include "math/math.hpp"

class MathServer : public ex::math {
    void lrpcTransmit(lrpc::span<const uint8_t> bytes) override {
        HAL_UART_Transmit(&huart1, bytes.data(), bytes.size(), HAL_MAX_DELAY);
    }
};

CalcService calc;
MathServer server;

int main() {
    hw_init();
    server.registerService(calc);

    while (true) {
        uint8_t byte;
        if (HAL_UART_Receive(&huart1, &byte, 1, 0) == HAL_OK) {
            server.lrpcReceive(byte);
        }
    }
}
```
